title: TabM: Advancing Tabular Deep Learning with Parameter-Efficient Ensembling
authors: Yury Gorishniy, Akim Kotelnikov, Artem Babenko (Yandex; HSE University)
tokenization_method: TabM itself contributes NO new tokenizer; its representation is a flat concatenation of per-feature embeddings fed to an MLP ensemble. By default: numerical features -> quantile normalization to a scalar; categorical features -> one-hot encoding; binary -> {0,1}; the concatenated vector goes straight into the MLP backbone. The contribution is the BACKBONE/ensembling, not the tokenizer. The reported best variant TabM-mini-dagger adds non-linear PIECEWISE-LINEAR EMBEDDINGS (PLE) (a slightly modified version of Gorishniy et al. 2022 'On Embeddings for Numerical Features'): each continuous feature is embedded into a de-dim vector, all per-feature embeddings are flattened, and that flat vector is the input to the ensembled MLP. So in this topic's vocabulary, TabM's effective 'token granularity' is per-feature embedding vectors that are immediately flattened+concatenated rather than kept as a token sequence.
feature_types_handled: numerical and categorical (mixed). Numerical via quantile-norm (+ optional PLE); categorical via one-hot; binary mapped to {0,1}. No native missing-value mechanism (assumes preprocessed input). No native temporal/time-varying handling: rows are treated as unordered static feature vectors even on the time-aware TabReD / domain-aware datasets.
token_granularity: per-feature embedding then FLATTENED into one long input vector (not a retained token sequence). With plain TabM, each feature is effectively a scalar slot; with TabM-dagger each continuous feature becomes a de-dim PLE embedding and all embeddings are concatenated. There is no [CLS] token and no attention over feature tokens — the 'mixing' of features happens inside the shared MLP weight matrix, not via per-token attention.
numerical_strategy: Default: quantile normalization (sklearn QuantileTransformer-style) to a single scalar per feature. Best-performing variant (dagger): modified PIECEWISE-LINEAR EMBEDDINGS (PLE) with #bins tuned in UniformInt[8,32]; the first multiplicative adapter R is re-initialized from N(0,1) when embeddings are used. Periodic embeddings (PLR / MLP-PLR style) are used for the retrieval baselines but TabM's reported flagship uses PLE.
categorical_strategy: one-hot encoding by default (no learned entity-embedding table inside TabM in the main protocol). Cardinalities are passed to the model so the input width accounts for the one-hot expansion. The official library also exposes learned categorical embeddings, but the paper's headline results use one-hot.
high_cardinality_handling: NONE specific. One-hot encoding is the default categorical treatment, which is exactly the wrong primitive for high-cardinality fields: input dimensionality (and the first MLP layer / first adapter R) grows linearly with total category count, inflating parameters and risking sparsity. The paper does not address high-cardinality categoricals; for a 70+-feature set with high-cardinality fields you must swap in learned embeddings / hashing / target encoding yourself before the TabM backbone.
temporal_handling: none. No patching, recurrence, or temporal-static fusion. The benchmark INCLUDES 9 domain-aware (incl. time-aware TabReD) splits and TabM still wins on them on average, but it consumes them as static rows — any temporal structure must be feature-engineered upstream.
scales_to_many_features: Strong and a central selling point. Because TabM is an MLP ensemble (flat-concat input, no self-attention), cost is LINEAR in feature count, avoiding FT-Transformer's O(k^2)-in-features attention and retrieval models' O(n)-in-dataset-size cost. Benchmark spans 3 to 986 features (Q50=20, mean=108); demonstrated on two large tables with 986 features / 6.5M objects and 103 features / 13M objects where attention (FT-T) is far slower and retrieval (TabR) goes OOM. The k=32 implicit ensemble adds only ~3d params per layer (BatchEnsemble adapters) and runs at well under x32 wall-clock due to parallel forward passes. Caveat: very wide/deep TabM accommodates more submodels, and too-narrow (d=64) / too-shallow (n=1) configs underperform.
downstream_model: MLP (N blocks of Linear-ReLU-Dropout + linear head) turned into a parameter-efficient ensemble of k=32 implicit MLPs via BatchEnsemble-style adapters; predictions are the mean over the k submodels at inference. Variants: TabM-packed (k independent MLPs, no sharing), TabM-naive (BatchEnsemble), TabM-mini (only the first multiplicative adapter R kept), TabM (all 3N adapters, non-first multiplicative adapters init to 1).
pretraining_objective: none. Fully supervised, trained from scratch per dataset with per-dataset hyperparameter tuning (Optuna) and multi-seed averaging. No self-supervised or transfer objective. Authors note TabM could be reused as a feature extractor but flag that combining the k object embeddings into one is unsolved.
key_results: On 46 public datasets (28 regression, 18 classification; 37 random + 9 domain-aware splits) under a shared tuning+multi-seed protocol: TabM-mini-dagger ranks best among ALL tabular DL models (mean rank 1.7 +/- 1.2), ahead of TabM (2.8), TabR-PLR (2.9), ModernNCA-PLR (3.0), CatBoost (3.2), XGBoost (3.3), LightGBM (3.4), MLP-dagger (3.8), and well ahead of attention models FT-T (4.6), SAINT (4.9), T2G (4.1), ExcelFormer (5.2) and plain MLP (5.5). Relative-improvement-over-MLP: TabM +2.15%, TabM-mini-dagger +2.92%, TabM-dagger-x5 (deep ensemble of TabM) +3.20%. Key mechanism finding: TabM's k submodels are WEAK individually (best single submodel TabM[B] = -0.06% vs MLP, i.e. no better than one MLP) but STRONG collectively (+2.15%) -> evidence of genuine submodel diversity. Greedy post-hoc submodel pruning (TabM[G]) retains ~8.8/32 submodels at near-full accuracy (+2.02%).
baselines_compared: MLP, FT-Transformer, SAINT, T2G-Former, ExcelFormer (attention); TabR, ModernNCA (retrieval); XGBoost, LightGBM, CatBoost (GBDT); plus variants with PLE/periodic embeddings (MLP-dagger, MLP-PLR, TabR-PLR, MNCA-PLR). Internal ablation ladder: MLP, MLP x32 (deep ensemble), TabM-packed (Packed-Ensemble), TabM-naive (BatchEnsemble), TabM-bad (first adapter removed), TabM-mini (only first adapter), TabM (better init).
transferable_idea: Two separable ideas. (1) PRIMARY (tokenization-relevant): for many-feature tables, FLAT-CONCAT of per-feature PLE embeddings into a plain MLP beats both raw-scalar MLP and attention-token FT-Transformer, while staying LINEAR in feature count — i.e. you do NOT need a Transformer's per-token attention to get top accuracy on 70+ features; good per-feature numerical embeddings + a strong backbone suffice and scale. (2) ORTHOGONAL (the paper's actual contribution): parameter-efficient ensembling (k=32 BatchEnsemble adapters: x(1) the first multiplicative adapter R is the load-bearing one) is a near-free accuracy/regularization boost that can wrap ANY tokenizer/backbone, including a future heterogeneous-feature tokenizer.
limitations: Authors-stated: (a) MLP backbone is the simplest; advanced backbones (esp. with normalization layers) need extra per-submodel affine adapters (FiLM-Ensemble style) and are unexplored; (b) the 'final object embedding' is now k vectors, not one -> reusing TabM as a feature extractor / multi-task is unsolved; (c) k is fixed at 32 heuristically and too-large k can hurt; (d) too-narrow/too-shallow configs underperform. Topic-relevant gaps NOT addressed: no high-cardinality categorical strategy (one-hot only), no temporal/time-series fusion, no missing-value handling, no explicit feature-interaction or feature-selection layer.
pitfalls: 1) PRIMARY-ROUTE MISMATCH: TabM is filed under feature-interaction-selection but it does NOT do explicit feature interaction (no cross/attention layer) NOR feature selection. Its only tie to this route is that flat-concat MLP scales linearly in feature count, sidestepping the cost that motivates interaction/selection machinery. Do not cite TabM as a feature-interaction method. 2) TabM is NOT a tokenization method; its tokenizer is borrowed (one-hot + quantile-norm + optional PLE). The big numerical-embedding gain (TabM -> TabM-mini-dagger, +2.15% -> +2.92%) is attributable to PLE (Gorishniy 2022), NOT to ensembling. Disentangle credit. 3) One-hot for categoricals makes the headline setup unsuitable as-is for high-cardinality 70+-feature tables. 4) The 'beats GBDT' framing rests on heavy per-dataset Optuna tuning + multi-seed averaging; CatBoost/XGBoost are within ~1 rank and the domain-aware (shift) splits narrow the gap further (XGBoost is 2nd on the 9 domain-aware datasets, ahead of plain TabM). 5) Benchmark Q50 is only 20 features; the 'many features' claim leans on a few wide outliers (986, 103) rather than a sweep concentrated at 70+.
relevance_to_topic: High as a backbone/baseline and as evidence that good per-feature numerical embeddings + a scalable MLP ensemble can match or beat token-sequence Transformers on wide tables; low as a tokenizer (it borrows one, and uses high-cardinality-hostile one-hot). For the 70+-mixed-feature target, TabM is the recommended cheap, linear-cost backbone to drop a proper heterogeneous-feature tokenizer ON TOP of, not the tokenizer itself.
code_available: yes — official PyTorch package, pip install tabm; repo https://github.com/yandex-research/tabm (actively maintained, ~1.1k stars, v0.0.3 Aug 2025; end-to-end notebook; integrates rtdl_num_embeddings for PiecewiseLinearEmbeddings/PeriodicEmbeddings; accepts categorical cardinalities). Paper experiments under paper/.
